title: SEGMENT 5 — BACs, clone-by-clone, and whole-genome shotgun: the two strategies

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — BACs, clone-by-clone, and whole-genome shotgun: the two strategies
This is the most technically dense segment in the lecture, so I am going to slow down and use analogies aggressively.
Stick with me.
Here is the problem.
Sanger sequencing, even with the best automated ABI 3700 machines of 1998, can only read about seven hundred to nine hundred bases of contiguous DNA at a time.
The human genome is three billion bases.
So you cannot just take a chromosome and sequence it end-to-end — there is no machine on earth that can do that.
You have to break the genome into pieces small enough to sequence, then sequence the pieces, then computationally stitch the pieces back together based on overlapping ends.
The question is: how do you break it up, and how do you stitch it back together?
The public consortium chose what is called the clone-by-clone or hierarchical approach.
Here is the analogy.
Imagine you want to copy a giant book — three thousand pages — using a photocopier that only copies one page at a time.
The careful, slow way is: first, you make a chapter outline.
You number every chapter.
Then you photocopy chapter one, page by page, in order, and you reassemble chapter one.
Then chapter two.
And so on.
You always know where you are in the book.
That is clone-by-clone.

The public consortium first built a physical map of the human genome — a low-resolution map of large overlapping DNA fragments, each about 150,000 base pairs long, cloned into things called Bacterial Artificial Chromosomes, BACs.
A BAC is a circular plasmid-like vector that can hold and faithfully replicate a chunk of human DNA up to about 200 kilobases inside a bacterial cell.
You will remember plasmids from the previous lecture — BACs are the heavyweight industrial-scale cousins of plasmids, engineered to carry far larger inserts.
The consortium fingerprinted tens of thousands of BACs, figured out which ones overlapped, and built a tiling path — a minimum set of overlapping BACs that covered each chromosome.
Then, for each BAC in the tiling path, they shotgunned that one BAC — sequenced random fragments of it — and assembled the BAC's 150,000 bases as a self-contained unit.
Then they stitched the assembled BACs together along the tiling path.
The advantages: every assembled BAC is anchored to a known physical location on a known chromosome.

Repetitive DNA — and remember, half the human genome is repetitive — is contained within the small space of a single BAC, so repeats from chromosome 1 cannot get confused with repeats from chromosome 14.
The assembly is local, controlled, and verifiable.
The disadvantages: it is slow.
Building the BAC map alone took years.
Every step has overhead.
And BACs are not free to make — each one is a cloned construct in bacteria, with all the labor of plasmid prep.
Celera chose the opposite approach: whole-genome shotgun.
Here is the shotgun analogy.
Take the same giant three-thousand-page book.
Now feed it through a paper shredder — not once but multiple times, at different shred sizes.
You now have a giant pile of confetti — millions of overlapping fragments.
You read every confetto.
Then you computationally figure out how the confetti pieces overlap, and you reassemble the book purely from overlap information, with no chapter outline at all.
That is whole-genome shotgun.
You take the whole genome, fragment it randomly, sequence millions of small fragments, and let a computer figure out how they fit together.
No physical map, no BACs, no tiling path.
Just sequence and assemble.
The advantages: it is fast.
You skip the entire map-building phase.
The wet-lab pipeline is simpler and more parallelizable.
The disadvantages: the assembly problem becomes nightmarish, especially for a repeat-rich genome like human.
If you have ten thousand nearly identical copies of a transposable element scattered across the genome, how does the computer know which copy goes where?

The answer, for Celera, turned out to be that you needed paired-end reads — sequence both ends of a longer fragment, separated by a known distance — and you needed very deep coverage, six to ten times the genome size in total bases sequenced.
And here is a fact that Celera was quiet about at the time but is now widely acknowledged: Celera's assembly used the public consortium's data.
Because the public data was being deposited daily into GenBank under the Bermuda Principles — that is the next segment — Celera was able to anchor its shotgun assembly against the public BAC map.
Some analyses suggest Celera could not have produced a usable assembly without the public data.
The public project, working clone by clone, could have finished without Celera.
The reverse is not clearly true.
The point of this segment is not that one approach was right and the other was wrong.
The point is that the two approaches were complementary, and the existence of both — the rivalry between them — pushed each side to be better.
The clone-by-clone approach gave us a reliably anchored backbone.
The shotgun approach proved that you could industrialize sequencing at a scale nobody had imagined.

Modern next-generation sequencing is shotgun on steroids — every Illumina run is whole-genome shotgun, with billions of short reads, assembled or aligned by descendants of the algorithms Celera pioneered.
The shotgun approach won the technological future.
The clone-by-clone approach won the 2003 finished sequence.
Both were necessary.
